cards.q: Define neonatal jaundice.
cards.a: Yellowish discoloration of skin, sclera and mucous membranes in a newborn due to raised serum bilirubin (hyperbilirubinaemia).

cards.q: At roughly what serum bilirubin level does jaundice become clinically visible?
cards.a: About 85 micromol/L (5 mg/dL).

cards.q: What is bilirubin a breakdown product of?
cards.a: Haem, mostly from senescent red blood cells.

cards.q: What is the newborn red cell lifespan compared to an adult's?
cards.a: 70–90 days in the newborn versus 120 days in the adult.

cards.q: Which hepatic enzyme conjugates bilirubin, and what is its state at birth?
cards.a: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UGT); it is immature/low at birth.

cards.q: Why does increased enterohepatic circulation raise bilirubin in newborns?
cards.a: Gut beta-glucuronidase deconjugates bilirubin, which is then reabsorbed into the blood.

cards.q: Define physiological jaundice by its time course.
cards.a: Appears after 24 hours, peaks day 3–5, resolves by day 14; unconjugated and benign.

cards.q: List the four features that make jaundice pathological.
cards.a: Onset within the first 24 hours; rapid rise (>85 micromol/L/day); raised conjugated fraction; persistence beyond 14 days.

cards.q: Give the causes of jaundice presenting within the first 24 hours.
cards.a: Haemolysis (Rh or ABO incompatibility, G6PD deficiency) and congenital infection.

cards.q: Give causes of jaundice presenting between 24 hours and 14 days.
cards.a: Physiological, breastfeeding jaundice, sepsis, polycythaemia, and bruising/cephalhaematoma.

cards.q: Give causes of prolonged jaundice (>14 days).
cards.a: Breast-milk jaundice, hypothyroidism, biliary atresia, UTI, and galactosaemia.

cards.q: Which prolonged-jaundice cause is a surgical emergency, and why?
cards.a: Biliary atresia — it is conjugated (obstructive) and needs surgery within the first weeks to preserve the liver.

cards.q: What is Kramer's rule?
cards.a: Jaundice progresses cephalocaudally — face first, then trunk, then limbs.

cards.q: What does yellowing of the palms and soles indicate?
cards.a: Severe jaundice (high bilirubin level).

cards.q: Why must bilirubin be measured rather than judged by eye?
cards.a: Visual assessment is unreliable with darker skin, artificial light, and anaemia; confirm with transcutaneous or serum bilirubin.

cards.q: What is kernicterus?
cards.a: Permanent brain injury from unconjugated bilirubin deposition in the basal ganglia — choreoathetoid cerebral palsy, sensorineural hearing loss and gaze palsy.

cards.q: Why can unconjugated (not conjugated) bilirubin cause kernicterus?
cards.a: It is fat-soluble and crosses the immature blood–brain barrier; conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble and cannot.

cards.q: List features of acute bilirubin encephalopathy.
cards.a: Lethargy, poor feeding, high-pitched cry, hypertonia and opisthotonus.

cards.q: How does phototherapy work and what wavelength is used?
cards.a: Blue light at 450–460 nm photoisomerises bilirubin into water-soluble forms excreted without conjugation.

cards.q: When is exchange transfusion indicated?
cards.a: Dangerously high or rapidly rising bilirubin, or signs of encephalopathy.

cards.q: What is the role of IV immunoglobulin in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: In isoimmune haemolytic disease it saturates Fc receptors, slowing antibody-mediated red cell destruction and bilirubin production.
